data.title: Netflix Stock Falls on Weak Guidance. Founder Reed Hastings Is Leaving the Board. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Netflix needs to prove that engagement is strong and content spending will pay off in its latest earnings report, now that the pursuit of Warner Bros. Discovery has come to an end.

Netflix is scheduled to report first-quarter financial results after the stock market closes on Thursday. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect the streaming giant to post adjusted earnings of 76 cents a share on revenue of $12.2 billion.

It's been a very busy time for Netflix over the past few months. The company announced in December that it had agreed to buy Warner Bros. studios and HBO Max. This set off a bidding war with Paramount Skydance, which was also highly interested in taking over the studio responsible for Harry Potter and Game of Thrones.

Netflix shareholders weren't excited about the deal, and the stock took a hit. Shares have since bounced back after the company announced in February it was backing out of the bidding war by not matching Paramount's latest offer. Eric Clark, chief investment officer at Accuvest Global Advisors, says now Netflix can focus on delivering what shareholders care most about.

"Now that the WBD deal is behind them, investors can get back to what matters most: content strategy, pricing levers and guidance, ad-tier growth, any new ways to drive viewership totals," he wrote on Wednesday.

Growing viewership is key for Netflix. The company no longer reports subscriber numbers, but Wall Street tracks viewership in other ways, including its biannual engagement report. The streamer has already seen exceptional growth and needs to prove to investors that it isn't slowing down. That's especially important as the economic environment becomes shaky, with rising inflation risking consumer spending on non-essentials, like entertainment.

Having a lower-priced ad-tier is a positive for Netflix, as the company can prevent users from cancelling outright if they are feeling economically pressured. However, Netflix announced in March that it was raising prices again.

Netflix needs to convince people to stay on the platform. One way to do that is by hosting exciting content — that's not cheap.

"With all the geopolitical uncertainty, I'm not sure management will do a big guide but I think we should expect them to re-focus everyone's attention on their content spending goals," Clark wrote.

Greg Silverman, global director of brand economics at Interbrand, notes that strong content curation is important as Netflix faces intensifying competition from YouTube and live TV platforms, along with possible consumer pushback on price hikes.

"To sustain its momentum, the company will need to continue innovating across content, technology, and user experience," he said.

Netflix stock has risen 14% this year.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Netflix Shares Slide After 2Q Outlook Misses Wall Street's Expectations
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Shares of Netflix declined after the streaming giant's outlook for the current quarter missed analyst expectations.

The stock fell 9% to $98.40 in after-hours trading on Thursday. Through market close, shares are up 15% year to date.

For the second quarter, the company forecast earnings per share of 78 cents on revenue of $12.57 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast earnings of 84 cents a share and revenue of $12.64 billion.

Netflix also backed its full-year guidance, and said it continues to expect content amortization growth to be weighted in the first half of the year, weighing on its operating margin in the second quarter.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Peruvian stocks recoup some losses while focus remains on Mideast resolution
data.description:
Key points:
  • Peruvian assets rebound after election-driven selloff, uncertainty persists
  • Brazil raises 5 billion euros in bonds; companies announce new debt sales
  • Brazil's economic activity index beats forecasts in March The World Bank plans up to $2 billion guarantee to help Argentina refinance debt

By Johann M Cherian and Twesha Dikshit

Peruvian stocks rose on Thursday, steadying after a sharp selloff in the previous session on election uncertainties, while assets across Latin America were muted as investors awaited more clarity on potential talks to end the Middle East conflict.

MSCI's index tracking equities in the copper producer (.MIPE00000PUS) edged up 3.7%, recouping some of its losses on Wednesday and a U.S.-listed exchange traded fund (.EPU) rose 2%, while the sol was little changed after the previous session's 1.6% slide.

However, international bonds were marginally lower on Thursday, reflecting investor nervousness that left-wing congressman Roberto Sanchez was narrowing his gap with market-friendly frontrunner Keiko Fujimori in a tight presidential race. A runoff is scheduled for June 7.

Sanchez, a former cabinet minister under ousted leftist ex-President Pedro Castillo, has supported stronger state control over natural resources, proposed reviewing mining and gas contracts, taxing windfall profits and imposing a wealth tax.

"Sanchez has indicated that he won't be as market friendly in terms of policy ideas, so overall, that situation is (fueling uncertainty) in terms of what will happen to the central bank and checks and balances of the economy," said Andrés Abadía, chief Latam economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

The possibility of a less-market friendly candidate at the helm of the country weighed on sentiment on Wednesday. The share index fell over 7%, marking its biggest daily drop since July 2021 - the month when Castillo was elected as the country's president.

MIDEAST JITTERS PREVAIL

Following several sessions of gains on the back of potential talks to end the Middle East conflict, investors were taking a pause. Both MSCI's indexes tracking Latam stocks (.MILA00000PUS) and currencies (.MILA00000CUS) were flat.

The indexes have recouped all losses since the conflict started in late February, with the currencies gauge also hitting a record high earlier in the week.

Israel and Lebanon agreed to begin a 10-day ceasefire following talks this week brokered by Washington, signalling a pause in Israel's conflict with Iran-backed Hezbollah.

Still tensions lingered as the question of Tehran's nuclear ambitions remained unresolved, while slow shipments through the Strait of Hormuz kept crude prices a hand's reach away from $100 a barrel and Iran flagged that

Brazil's equities dipped 0.5%, while the country's international bonds (BR018426994=), (105756CE8=) were steady.

The country said it raised a record 5 billion euros ($5.9 billion) with a bond sale on Wednesday, adding to the $4.5 billion sale of dollar-denominated bonds it issued in early February. The real was little changed.

It would likely fuel volatility this year ahead of general elections in October. The country's benchmark interest rates are among the highest in the world at 14.75%.

The region's largest economy will announce new measures to mitigate the economic impact of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, including steps to curb rises in gasoline prices, the Institutional Relations Minister said.

Several Brazilian companies also announced bond sales including meatpacker Minerva <BEEF3.SA>, lender Banco do Brasil <BBAS3.SA>, according to IFR, and holding firm J&F Investimentos, according to newspaper Valor.

On the data front, the country's economic activity index, a proxy for gross domestic product, rose a seasonally adjusted 0.6% in February from the previous month, surpassing economists' expectations.

Elsewhere, the pesos of Chile and Mexico were steady, while Colombia's edged up 0.4%.

The World Bank Group said it was working on a guarantee of up to $2 billion to help refinance Argentina's debt. The local peso strengthened 0.3%.

International bonds were marginally higher, with those maturing in 2035 and 2038 (US040114HT09=TE), (US040114HU71=TE) up as much as 0.5 cents on the dollar, according to Tradeweb data.

A rise in crude prices lifted shares of Brazil's state-owned energy company, Petrobras and Colombia's Ecopetrol by over 4% each.

data.title: U.S. Stocks Finish Lackluster Session Moderately Higher
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Stocks fluctuated over the course of the trading session on Thursday but largely maintained a positive bias before ending the day moderately higher. The Nasdaq and the S&P 500 added to the strong gains posted over the few days, reaching new record closing highs.

The major averages all finished the day in positive territory. The Nasdaq climbed 86.69 points or 0.4 percent to 24,102.70, the S&P 500 rose 18.33 points or 0.3 percent to 7,041.28 and the Dow increased 115.00 points or 0.2 percent to 48,578.72.

Stocks continued to benefit from the upward momentum seen over the past few weeks, which has helped the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 more than offset the nosedive seen in the weeks after the start of the U.S. war with Iran.

Traders also continued to express optimism about a second round of peace talks between the U.S. and Iran, although a new meeting has not been officially announced.

According to reports, the warring nations are considering extending the ceasefire by two weeks in order to allow more time for talks.

Adding to the positive sentiment, President Donald Trump announced in a post on Truth Social that Israel and Lebanon have agreed to a 10-day ceasefire.

Trump also said he has invited Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun to the White House for peace talks.

Iran has been demanding that Israel halt its on attacks Hezbollah in Lebanon as part of the current ceasefire.

'It's like the events of the past month-and-a-half have been placed in the rearview mirror by investors,' said Dan Coatsworth, head of markets at AJ Bell. 'The market's sanguine perspective may be tested if the rhetoric about an end to the fighting isn't matched by reality sooner rather than later.'

In U.S. economic news, the Federal Reserve released a report showing industrial production in the U.S. unexpectedly decreased in the month of March

The Fed said industrial production fell by 0.5 percent in March after climbing by 0.7 percent in February. Economists had expected industrial production to inch up by 0.1 percent compared to the 0.2 percent increase originally reported for the previous month.

The unexpected decrease in industrial production partly reflected significant pullbacks in utilities and mining output.

Sector News

Transportation stocks moved sharply higher on the day, driving the Dow Jones Transportation Average up by 4.1 percent to a record closing high.

J.B. Hunt (JBHT) helped lead the sector higher, with the transportation and logistics company spiking by 6.3 percent after reporting better than expected first quarter results.

Significant strength was also visible among telecom stocks, as reflected by the 3.8 percent surge by the NYSE Arca North American Telecom Index.

Networking, computer hardware, software and oil producer stocks also saw considerable strength, while airline stocks showed a notable move to the downside.

Other Markets

In overseas trading, stock markets across the Asia-Pacific region moved mostly higher during trading on Thursday. Japan's Nikkei 225 Index surged by 2.4 percent to a record high, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index shot up by 1.7 percent.

The major European markets turned in a mixed performance on the day. While the French CAC 40 Index edged down by 0.1 percent, the U.K.'s FTSE 100 Index rose by 0.3 percent and the German DAX Index climbed by 0.4 percent.

In the bond market, treasuries extended the pullback seen over the course of the previous session. Subsequently, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, increased 2.7 basis points to 4.309 percent.

data.title: Schwab Is Ready to Let Customers Trade Bitcoin. How Its Fees Stack Up Versus Robinhood, Fidelity. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Investors will soon be able to directly trade and hold cryptocurrencies Bitcoin and Ethereum at Charles Schwab. The brokerage firm had already announced it would provide such a spot trading capability, but on Thursday it unveiled details of the launch.

The brokerage firm said it would roll out Schwab Crypto, as the offering is called, gradually in coming weeks. It will allow clients to view and trade cryptocurrency and traditional investments side-by-side across Schwab's trading platforms, including thinkorswim.

Bitcoin and Ethereum are the two largest cryptocurrencies, and Schwab says they together make up three-quarters of the crypto market capitalization. Over time, Schwab intends to add other cryptocurrencies to its offering.

The company says it will charge 0.75%, or 75 basis points, on the dollar value of each crypto trade. The company won't charge clients to hold crypto at Schwab.

Customers will maintain a separate crypto account through Schwab Crypto, and Charles Schwab Premier Bank will serve as the custodian of clients' digital assets. Paxos, a regulated blockchain infrastructure provider, will deliver subcustody and trade execution services, according to Schwab.

The company has been able to expand into cryptocurrency because of regulatory changes under the Trump administration that have made it easier for banks to hold digital assets.

However, Schwab customers have long had access to cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds. Indeed, the company says clients hold approximately 20% of spot crypto exchanged-traded products. But customers have been asking for the ability to directly hold crypto, and do so via other companies, according to Schwab. The new crypto offering may spur customers to consolidate their crypto assets at Schwab.

"They've been begging us to launch this so they can move their crypto assets to us," CEO Rick Wurster said during his company's earnings call on Thursday morning.

He said customers want to hold crypto at Schwab because they trust the company with their other assets and they want to simplify their financial lives by consolidating assets and accounts at a single company.

Schwab reported first-quarter earnings that showed robust growth in asset-gathering and new account openings, but the stock sold off, with shares falling 7.5%.

Some other brokerage firms, such as Robinhood and Fidelity Investments, offer customers the ability to directly hold and trade crypto assets. Fidelity's retail crypto offering, Fidelity Crypto, launched in 2023. The company allows customers to trade a variety of cryptocurrencies and it changes a trading fee of 1%. Robinhood charges fees ranging from 0.03% to 0.85% depending on a client's trading volume.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: First Financial Bankshares Q1 net income rises
data.description:
Overview

  • US regional bank's Q1 net income rose 17% yr/yr on higher net interest income

  • Q1 diluted EPS increased to $0.50 from $0.43 a year earlier

  • Company cites core balance sheet growth and lower funding costs for improved results

Outlook

  • Company did not provide specific guidance for the current or future quarters

Result Drivers

  • NET INTEREST INCOME - Higher net interest income and margin, aided by a $1.26 mln reversal of interest expense, contributed to improved results

  • NONINTEREST INCOME - Growth in trust fees from higher trust asset values and increased mortgage income from higher origination volume and better margins supported results

  • HIGHER OPERATING COSTS - Noninterest expenses rose, mainly due to increased staff costs and higher software and professional fees

data.title: Alcoa Q1 revenue, adjusted EPS miss estimates
data.description:

  • U.S. aluminum producer's Q1 revenue and adjusted EPS missed analyst expectations

Outlook

data.title: Netflix shatters profit expectations thanks to price increase and $2.8 billion breakup fee from Warner Bros.
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

Founder Reed Hastings won't stand for re-election to board after his term expires

Netflix on Thursday reported its first earnings since ending its unsuccessful effort to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery's studio and streaming businesses for $82.7 billion.

Netflix shattered profit expectations in the first quarter, the company reported Thursday, attributing its large gains to a recent price increase and the $2.8 billion breakup fee it received when its deal to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery collapsed.

The streaming giant (NFLX) reported net income of $5.28 billion, compared to $2.89 billion in the same quarter a year earlier. Analysts had estimated net income of $3.29 billion, according to FactSet.

Earnings per share came in at $1.23, the company reported, nearly double the 66 cents it reported in the same quarter a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet had predicted EPS of 76 cents per shares.

Revenue rose to $12.25 billion, up from $10.5 billion in the first quarter of 2025. That came in ahead of the $12.2 billion that analysts had predicted.

Netflix maintained its full-year forecast of $50.7 billion to $51.7 billion in revenue, and a full-year operating margin of 31.5%, up from 29.5% last year.

The company also announced that its founder Reed Hastings would not stand for re-election to its board of directors after his term expires in June.

The earnings report was the company's first since it ended its fight in February to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery's studio and streaming businesses for $82.7 billion. The merger effort had dismayed investors, who sent Netflix shares tumbling as much as 30%.

Netflix backed out of the bidding in February after rival Paramount Skydance (PSKY) came in with a higher offer of $110 billion for all of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), including its declining television division.

Since then, Wall Street has turned more bullish on Netflix's stock, which has risen 42% to its highest point since early December, when it had initially reached an agreement with Warner Bros.

Investors are now more focused on signs of how Netflix intends to spur growth going forward. While there have been signs that engagement from viewers has slowed over the past year, analysts have pointed to recent price increases and Netflix's efforts to develop an advertising business as potential engines for revenue growth this year.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Disciplined Growth Acquisition Corp. Files for IPO
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Disciplined Growth Acquisition Corp., a Cayman Islands-based blank check company, filed for an initial public offering.

The company said it intends to offer 15 million units at $10 each, with each unit containing one share and a right to one-tenth of a share following the company's initial business combination.

The company also said its sponsor, Maxim Group, and other third-party investors had agreed to purchase an additional 300,000 units for $10 each in a private placement.

Disciplined Growth expects to raise $150 million from the IPO and $3 million from the private placement. The full exercise of over-allotment options could raise an additional $22.7 million.

The company said it has not selected any target for a potential business combination and could pursue targets in any business or industry, though it is focusing on the financial technology and aerospace and defense sectors.

It intends to apply to list its shares and units on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbols DGAC and DGACU, respectively.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: SUMA Acquisition Announces Separate Trading of Class A Shares and Rights, Symbols SUMA & SUMAR
data.description:
SUMA Acquisition announced that holders may separately trade Class A ordinary shares and rights beginning April 20, 2026.

Key Highlights:

  • Commencing April 20, 2026, holders of IPO units may elect to separate and trade Class A ordinary shares and Rights.
  • No fractional Rights will be issued; only whole Rights will trade after separation.
  • Separated Class A ordinary shares and Rights are expected to trade on Nasdaq under symbols SUMA and SUMAR.
  • Units not separated will continue trading on Nasdaq under the symbol SUMAU.
  • Holders must have brokers contact Continental Stock Transfer & Trust Company to effect unit separation.

Original SEC Filing: SUMA Acquisition Corp [ SUMAU ] - 8-K - Apr. 16, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: FFB Bancorp Q1 EPS falls on lower gain on sale of loans, investment income
data.description:
Overview

  • California community bank's Q1 net income and EPS fell sharply yr/yr, but rose sequentially

  • Q1 operating revenue down 20% yr/yr, driven by lower gain on sale of loans and investment income

  • Company repurchased $5.38 mln in stock and redeemed $281,000 in subordinated debentures

Outlook

  • Company expects deposit growth to increase over the remainder of the year, reducing wholesale funding reliance

  • FFB Bancorp anticipates operating leverage to improve, driving the efficiency ratio lower in coming quarters

  • Company is focused on right-sizing key performance metrics and resolving up to $10 mln in nonperforming loans

Result Drivers

  • LOWER GAIN ON SALE OF LOANS AND INVESTMENT INCOME - Co said operating revenue decline was mainly due to lower gain on sale of loans and investment income

  • NET INTEREST MARGIN - NIM rose 5 bps sequentially as loan repricing and growth outpaced funding cost increases, per CFO Bhavneet Gill

  • MERCHANT SERVICES REVENUE - Merchant services revenue decreased due to planned ISO partner exits and lower payment volumes, but net merchant services income rose 13% sequentially

Company press release:

data.title: Netflix keeps 2026 revenue forecast unchanged; Chairman Reed Hastings to exit board
data.description:
Overview

  • Streaming platform's Q1 revenue grew 16% yr/yr, slightly beating analyst expectations

  • Adjusted EPS for Q1 rose 86% yr/yr, boosted by $2.8 bln termination fee from Warner Bros deal

  • Netflix's Chairman Reed Hastings to exit board when his term expires in June

Outlook

  • Netflix sees Q2 revenue at $12,574 mln, up 13.5% year-over-year

  • Company expects Q2 operating margin of 32.6%, down from 34.1% in Q2'25

  • Netflix maintains full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $50.7-$51.7 bln and margin of 31.5%

Result Drivers

  • MEMBERSHIP GROWTH - Co said higher-than-forecasted membership growth was a primary driver of Q1 revenue and profit

  • WBD TERMINATION FEE - Higher-than-expected diluted EPS was driven by the $2.8 bln termination fee related to the Warner Bros transaction

  • ENTERTAINMENT EXPANSION - Co cited expanded entertainment offerings, including new content and live events, as boosting engagement and retention

Key Details

Metric

Beat/Miss

*Applies to a deviation of less than 1%; not applicable for per-share numbers.

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 41 "strong buy" or "buy", 12 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the online services peer group is "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Netflix Inc is $115.00, about 6.8% above its April 15 closing price of $107.71

  • The stock recently traded at 32 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 29 three months ago
